transcript: Humans are filling up the space around Earth with trash. Decades of space exploration that began with a single satellite launch in 1957 have left discarded rocket bodies, mission-related debris, and thousands of satellites, many of them long abandoned and no longer functional, crisscrossing at high speeds just above Earth's atmosphere. And with a booming space industry launching more objects into orbit than ever before, this crowding is steadily increasing the probability of catastrophic in-orbit collisions. Scientists worry that a runaway chain reaction of collisions and fragmentation could one day fill Earth's orbit with so much debris that space will become unusable to humans. It's why the world's leading space agencies and some for-profit companies are working on big ideas to fix our space debris problem. From trash collecting spacecraft armed with harpoons and nets, to a giant cloud of metal dust. And there's one solution that's been proposed for years to halt these dangerous bits of garbage in their tracks lasers because if we get creative and start addressing this problem right now we can still fix it before it's too late the vast majority of human-made objects surrounding earth are here in a region called low earth orbit it's where we have communication satellites plus spacecraft like the international space station and our massive amount of space trash the more objects we put in space means more chances these objects smash into each other break apart and multiply and all it takes is a handful of fragmentation events to dramatically increase the total number of objects orbiting Earth. Like in 2009, when a defunct Russian satellite accidentally collided with a commercial satellite, exploding into thousands of trackable fragments. And in 2007, when China intentionally destroyed one of its own satellites in a weapons test, which also resulted in tens of thousands of fragments of varying sizes of debris. A theoretical cascade effect called the Kessler syndrome introduced in the 1970s warned that if enough incidents like these occur, the fragments of debris they create could trigger an uncontrollable number of catastrophic collisions, exponentially increasing the field of debris around Earth. But we can still fix this before it's too late, and there's a few ways it could happen. The way to get rid of most space debris is to slow it down enough to fall out of orbit and into Earth's atmosphere, where it burns up and is destroyed. But different-sized space junk requires different approaches to do this. The biggest bits of space debris measure more than 10 centimeters in size. This includes dead satellites, rocket bodies, and big pieces of debris created during space missions. These are large enough to be tracked from Earth, and scientists are developing tiny spacecraft that can reach these objects and slow them down, using various methods to either push or pull them, guiding them down to Earth's atmosphere to be destroyed. Commercial companies like Europe's Clear Space and Japan's Astroscale are working with global space agencies on this tech, hoping to demonstrate the removal of large debris sometime in the next decade. The two smaller classifications of space debris mostly come from fragmentation events, like explosions and collisions. Their numbers are estimates because we still don't have the technology in place to reliably track debris below 10 centimeters in size. The tiniest pieces include flecks of paint and metal that, even at a fraction of a millimeter in size, regularly cause minor damage to spacecraft. So far, scientists have proposed two main ideas to remove these. One, a physical sweeper placed in orbit that could catch or slow them down enough to deorbit them. But this approach for removal, at least as far as NASA is concerned, is too expensive to even consider. The other, more cost-effective solution would be to release a cloud of tungsten dust on top of the debris, which would coat the objects and weigh them down, dragging them into Earth's atmosphere to be destroyed. On the other hand, dumping a 20-ton cloud of heavy metal dust into Earth's orbit could end up contributing to the pollution of space. So, it hasn't been tried yet. As of right now, the sweeper and tungsten dust ideas probably aren't practical solutions. But there's another option for this class of space debris in the middle. It presents a unique problem, because at 1 to 10 centimeters, this debris is too small to track, but big enough to do serious serious damage just one fragment even one centimeter in size moving at an average speed of seven kilometers per second is powerful enough to knock out a satellite analysis from NASA shows that focusing on this range of debris would be highly effective in managing the space debris problem as a whole this is where the lasers come in lasers can direct a beam of energy and apply force to an object without ever making physical contact. They could be used to nudge a fast-moving piece of small debris and disrupt its orbit, either knocking it into higher, less crowded levels of orbit or lowering it into Earth's atmosphere to be destroyed. This method is sometimes referred to as a laser broom a ground-based laser system was proposed by nasa's project orion in 1996 and according to nasa's 2023 cost and benefit analysis of space debris removal methods a ground or space-based laser system would be the most cost-effective way to remove this class of one to ten centimeter size debris but historically not everyone has loves this. People were resistant to the idea of lasers in space because all the spy satellites are worried they might get blinded. But now they're starting to come around. Claude Phipps was a part of Project Orion back in the 90s and is who originally proposed this idea in the U.S. to deal with space debris using lasers. It's a fairly simple idea. A ground-based laser for debris removal would basically work like this. First, a radar system would detect a small piece of debris coming over the horizon and lock onto its position. Because while these objects are too small to track when out of sight, they can be sufficiently tracked as they shoot across the sky long enough to aim the telescope that focuses the laser and fire a near instantaneous pulse of energy through the atmosphere with remarkable precision a space-based laser would work similarly with a couple key differences one advantage of being in orbit is that these lasers would have a more favorable angle to shoot debris downward into Earth's atmosphere and they wouldn't need to correct for Earth's atmosphere or long distances when aiming, like a ground-based system would, making a space-based solution potentially more efficient. But being in space, it wouldn't have access to Earth's power grid and would be much more difficult to maintain. The laser on the ground is going to be cheaper, but the geometry is kind of screwed up. If you're on the ground, you're having to shoot it this way. Whereas in space, you can see everywhere, you know, and hundreds of thousands of these things are available when you're in orbit. Shoot this one, shoot that one, shoot the other one as often as you want. That same NASA cost and benefit report suggested that a laser system could be highly effective for dealing with large debris, too, by nudging the debris out of the way of a spacecraft's orbit when an incoming collision is detected. This method would be more cost-effective than sending spacecraft to retrieve the debris and potentially safer than dragging it back into Earth's atmosphere, since especially large pieces of debris aren't guaranteed to be destroyed and could pose a threat to people on the ground. Claude told me that we have the technology now to develop ground and space-based lasers to fight space debris. But there's one big thing that's standing in the way. It all comes down to money. It's not a question of knowing what to do, it's a question of people committing to it. There are a couple commercial companies around the world working on it, and we may not be so far away from a future where ground and space-based laser systems are sweeping the night sky of space debris. There's one more problem we need to confront before we start cleaning up space, though. Right now, international space law forbids anybody from touching an object in space that it didn't launch. So even if a Japanese company like X-Fusion or NASA do develop a laser broom, they couldn't use it on a dead Russian satellite, for example. The lawyers have to work that out. And where there's a will, there's a way. That's the way I look at it. Cleaning up this mess is going to require an international effort and cooperation from commercial actors like SpaceX. But removing what's up there isn't the only way to deal with the problem. The European Space Agency's Zero Debris Charter, announced in June 2023, emphasizes mitigation of space debris, preventing the creation of new debris going forward, by committing to things like designing satellites that re-enter Earth's atmosphere on their own after completing their missions, instead of sitting in space as trash. In the U.S., the FCC introduced a new five-year rule in September 2022 that said U.S.-based spacecraft put into low-Earth orbit must de-orbit themselves within five years of mission's end. Efforts like these are just the beginning, but a combination of remediation and mitigation efforts is projected to make serious progress in battling our space debris problem, if everyone using space as a resource can change their habit of littering before it's too late. Before you go, we have something we're excited to share with you. It's a special video coming out in March for members of our Vox Contributions Program. The Vox Contributions Program helps fund our journalism through direct support from viewers like you. As a thank you to our recurring contributors, our video team put together an exclusive video tutorial in which our art director, Joey, will walk you through the process of making the classic Vox document highlight animation. Head over to vox.com slash give now to become a contributor, and keep an eye out for that video in the coming weeks. Thanks for watching.
